title: Tartiflette
subtitle: Savoy potato gratin with reblochon and lardons.
excerpt: Sliced potatoes baked with smoked bacon, soft onion, white wine, and a whole half-wheel of reblochon laid cut-side down over the top. Skiing food. A 45-minute oven dish that feeds four through a winter evening.
sourceNotes: Mid-twentieth-century invention by the Reblochon producers' union to popularise the cheese, drawing on the older Savoyard péla. The technique here follows the cooperative's documented version.

content.text: Sliced potatoes parboiled until almost tender, lardons fried with onion until both are gold, white wine bubbled away in the pan. The whole lot tipped into a dish, a half-wheel of reblochon laid cut-side down on top, the oven left to do the rest. The cheese sinks into the potatoes as it melts; the rind stays just dry enough to colour and crinkle.

Reblochon is the dish. Camembert and brie won't deliver the same molten interior; they lack the rind structure to hold and the saline edge that cuts the potato. If proper reblochon is hard to find, look for raclette, fontina, or taleggio as the working substitute.
What you need

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: waxy varieties like Charlotte or Désirée, peeled and sliced 5 mm thick

items.name: Camembert
items.prepNote: ideally a whole reblochon wheel; camembert, taleggio, or fontina work as substitute

content.text: A heavy 30 cm frying pan for the onion and bacon, and a 25 by 30 cm gratin dish or any wide oven dish that holds the layered potatoes in roughly two thicknesses.
Method
Parboil the potatoes

Bring a large pan of salted water to a rolling boil. Tip in {{potato}} of sliced potatoes and cook for 7 to 8 minutes, until the slices yield to a sharp knife but still hold their shape. Drain and tip into a wide bowl to stop them sticking. Heat the oven to 200°C.
Cook the lardons and onion

Set the frying pan over medium heat. Tip in {{streaky-bacon}} of lardons and fry for 5 minutes until the fat runs and the edges colour. Lift the bacon out into a bowl with a slotted spoon, leaving the fat in the pan. Add {{onion}} of sliced onions and a pinch of salt. Cook for 10 minutes, stirring often, until soft and golden. Stir in {{garlic}} of chopped garlic for the last minute.
Deglaze and combine

Pour {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine into the pan. Let it bubble down to almost nothing, 2 to 3 minutes. Return the bacon to the pan and pour in {{double-cream}} of double cream. Season with {{black-pepper}} of pepper and a small pinch of salt; the bacon and cheese both salt the dish. Take off the heat.
Assemble

Rub the inside of the baking dish with the cut side of the halved garlic clove. Lay half the parboiled potatoes across the base. Spoon over half the bacon-onion-cream mixture. Layer the rest of the potatoes on top, then the remaining bacon mixture. Halve the cheese horizontally so you have two thin rounds, rind on top. Lay them cut-side down on the potatoes so the soft cheese touches the food and the rind faces the oven.
Bake

Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the cheese rind is deep golden and bubbling around the edges, and the potatoes underneath are tender to the point of a knife. Rest for 5 minutes before serving so the cheese settles.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Slice the potatoes 5 mm thick, no thicker. Parboil for the full 7 to 8 minutes until they yield to a knife.
items.cause: Parboil too short, or slices too thick.

items.fix: Always lay the cut side down. The exposed rind on top should colour and crisp slightly.
items.cause: Cheese laid rind-down; rind acts as a barrier.
items.symptom: Cheese slid off the top without melting through

items.fix: Blanch the lardons in a pan of boiling water for 1 minute before frying. Pat dry and continue as before.
items.cause: Smoked streaky from a deli counter can be heavily cured.
items.symptom: Bacon too salty for the dish

items.fix: Cover loosely with foil for the first 15 minutes, then uncover for the final colour.
items.cause: Oven too hot or dish too shallow.
items.symptom: Top burnt before potatoes warmed through

content.text: Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Tartiflette doesn't freeze well; the texture of melted cheese collapses on reheat. Leftovers keep for two days in the fridge and warm through at 160°C, covered with foil, for 15 minutes. The assembled dish can wait, refrigerated and lidded, for up to 4 hours before baking, with the cheese added only at the last.
Where this dish lives

Tartiflette belongs to the high Alps, the Savoy mountains where reblochon is made. Ski-resort restaurants serve it in every chalet from Méribel to Val d'Isère as the late-afternoon dish that the slopes earn you. Drink something dry and Alpine alongside: an Apremont, a Roussette de Savoie. Green salad after, not before.
